but.

we can start with your side, then we will do the same for ours.

the chief of staff here, kind of helping the different areas of the business.

yeah, I'm Marcel, your CEO, co-founder, and I'm excited to get this thing going and a little context for these first eight weeks is ultimately it's...

Okay.

Yeah, so I guess the simple and long answer.
Thank Thank
Same time is that we're getting ready to do a more GA, general availability, launch in the middle of November.

Up until now, we've been in a limited beta where people who find out about auto can go to our basic website, which has almost no content on it, and can sign up for the beta by putting their email address in.
And so we've been running that for, I think, about six to eight months at this point.
We have hundreds of users, not thousands.
And so far, auto is doing what it's supposed to, and we feel pretty good about the product.
We're starting to get product market fit with people starting to want to come back to auto.
Auto autos gets better, know, the next time you come back, all of all the cycles are working.
But, you know, a big part of a launch is trying to let people know you exist, especially as a startup.

And so, you know, we have a couple of different avenues of either going and doing some B2B, but really we're targeting the actual employees who travel, who may not have a travel management company that they work with, and wanting to let them know that auto exists.

And so the option is to go build out all that content ourselves, to try to get authority in Google and especially in a lot of the AI tools, because I think there's a great overlap of people asking ChatGPT, how should I book this trip?
And then if ChatGPT responds back and says, hey, there's this company auto, well, that's, that'd be amazing.
And so building out that content, you know, is one of the, I'd say, core strategies for launch.
And, you know, talked to Marcelo, I think it was a month and a half, two months ago or so.
We were not in a position at all to do something like this.
But, you know, now coming up to it, I think it just makes more sense.
That's the net of YNL.
Yeah, that's perfect.

And Tero is one that we've been working with for a while who were very early, you know, and had no content almost at all.
man, we're building everything from the ground up.
So we'll definitely, like, gear everything to that piece, you know.
So, like, there's a couple of new ones, right, where we want to get it right.
But then when you're early on, we also want to make sure we're kind of balancing the speed quality and a little bit of volume because you just need to go a little bit longer.
When you're early on and don't have a lot of authority yet.
Yeah, for sure.
We're excited to accelerate this.
Absolutely.

So of these hundreds of users that you guys currently have, if we talk to some of these best customers that are coming back, maybe evolving with the product itself, what would they say you guys are uniquely good at?
You know, outside of being the best assistant.
I think some of the feedback I've gotten is, you know, and again, it's more product feedback, but it gives you a sense of it.
Are people saying, wow, it's pretty amazing.
You know, I stayed at this one hotel a couple months ago and Otto found it right away and listed that as the hotel suggestion.
You know, I think it's...

It really comes down to, again, the feedback I'm getting is, Otto just seems to know what airline and what flight I want, and seems to know what hotel I need to book, and just ask me if I want to book it, and when you say yes, it does.
That's kind of the net of it is that, you know, in many ways, as long as you don't have to interact with Otto very much, then it's successful.

I like to think of some of this, like, like, Uber in early days, where, you know, the big wow moment was when you would get out of the Uber, and there was no transaction, there was no kind of handing over money, at least, that was a big wow moment for me.
And, and they, you know, they kind of talk about it and say they got rid of checkout, right, there's, there's really no checkout.
Same thing with Otto, Otto is going to ask you, hey, do you want me to book this?
And as long as it has all your payment and credentials, you don't go through that rigmarole again.
You're not entering all your personal details in again.
You're not entering your loyalty numbers again.
It just books it.
Fantastic.

We'll get into this more on the product and the ICP side, but can you help me understand a little bit more about how you all get to know the customer's unique needs and preferences, like what that onboarding looks like?
It sounds like it's a pretty big differentiator for you all.

Yeah, so I won't take you on the full journey of how we got here, but in the beginning, were asking, Otto would go through and ask you a series of some 20 questions, and nobody wanted to go through that.
That was the opposite of product market fit.
People just wanted to go in and start using it.
And so we actually spent a bunch of time teaching Otto how to learn.
And so, you know, based on the searches you're doing, based on you saying, actually, show me the Delta flight show.
The Alaska Airlines flights, then Otto starts learning your preferences and starts, you know, saving that down.
When it gets a little confused or if it's not 100% sure this is a preference, he'll actually ask you and say, hey, I noticed that you, you know, you booked a king bed for the hotel.
you prefer king beds?
And then you can say, eh, I don't really care what type of bed it is.
Or you can say, yes, I prefer a king bed.

The other kind of, I wouldn't say secret sauce, but something that Otto does incredibly well is if we can get the user to connect their calendar, it's going to look back in the last two years, find all of your travel that you've done that most people save in their calendar and, you know, Gmail or any version of Google Workspace will look through your confirmation emails and put all those details in.
And based on that, it'll build up a profile of who you are as a traveler.
What time of day do you like traveling?
What are your favorite airlines?
What hotels have you stayed at?

If all those hotels end up being some fashion of Bonvoy hotels, you've stayed at the Residence Inn, the Courtyard, and Full Service Marriott, and the Ritz-Carlton, it will understand and know that you're a Bonvoy aficionado and you prefer the Bonvoy hotels.
So it has knowledge of the different hotel chains and things like that.
So that's really how it learns, is as you go, as you book, and getting signals from that.
But the ideal thing for us, and Otto will prompt users for this, is to connect the calendar.
Obviously, Otto has to earn the right to get access to the calendar as well.
Is there anything you guys are doing to build trust in between that kind of initial onboarding and asking for permission for calendar?
I think part of it is just demonstrating what Otto is doing, how it's searching and finding flights.
So...
there anything
And things like that.
then I think as soon as they do their first transaction, then you've earned that trust because they've obviously given you their credit card details and all that.

And that's, you know, that's the best moment to say for Otto to say, hey, can I get access to your calendar and then I'll store, you know, keep your travel plans up to date in your calendar and I'll learn more about you kind of a thing.
So, but before they booked, it's a fine line.
we're, frankly, we're trying to figure out when is the best time and how do we teach Otto when the best time is to ask for that.

Do you feel like, I'm curious, are there any restrictions on, maybe taking a step back, right, like from our early like sales conversations, I think it's really like the ICP at the time, we'll get into ICP, it was more kind of the busy maybe executive that travels a lot.
Is that still like the right, as you're talking about these things, that's still like the right mental model?
But we'll get into ICP, but just to frankly, you're asking these questions.
So I think it's, you almost use that busy exec as the example, but the reality is that, you know, the busy exec already has an executive assistant who's helping them book their travel.
And so, you know, some of the things that we put in some of the, you we've been running some Instagram little videos and ads and things like that.
And a couple of the videos, you know, we kind of keyed in on the language of, you know, the execs have, you know, the execs in your company have an executive assistant.
It's time for everyone in the company to get one.
And so, so the idea really is that, you know, the target audience for us here is anybody who travels a lot, who manages their own travel.
And so that's, that's a lot of, you know.
Got it.

Account reps and sales folks at startups.
It's a lot of lawyers.
It's a lot of account, you know, kind of finance type folks to work for smaller companies.
So, you know, the SME, SMB type market is an audience.

But then there's also what we found for the beta for sure is that there's this set of, you know, very early adopters who are traveling a lot, who may work for companies that have a travel management company, but they don't like that travel management company and want to try something different.
And so they come in and they try auto and want to transact that way.

And so I think those are the two where we've gotten the most interest from is really that, you know, account execs, consultants type folks who work for smaller companies that don't yet have a travel management company where the CEO or...
The CFO put together a rudimentary travel policy that says, hey, you know, when we fly, we're going to fly a coach unless it's over four hours and you get to fly a premium economy.
And hotels everywhere except for New York, London, and San Francisco, you know, max $300 and those cities $400.

Their basic travel policy, they're written as a PDF, something like that, but they're not engaged with a Navantrip Actions or any Concur or Amex Global Business Travel or anything like that because they're too small.
And by the way, that's at least half of the entire business travel market are people who work for companies like that, like I just described.
So it's a massive market.
Yeah, we learned a lot since we last talked as one of our newer customers is Engine.
Yeah, yeah.
And so I think there's a lot of different angles we can take with you.
Obviously, like, we wouldn't have the same team working on accounts.
Not that you guys are necessarily competitive at all.
But it's just, like, the topical learning there, you know.

So that's why I was asking because it's very important to nail down the type of travel and the type of audience that does that travel because there's things surrounding the booking itself, you know, not only the booking as a core, but then the other piece is that they are pretty restricted on the ability to mention hotels by name because of the way they do group bookings and stuff like that.

Whereas, like, I'm assuming you don't have any restrictions if we were to do, like, best hotels for this or that, which opens up a massive opportunity because that's the kind of content that's getting pulled into LLM.
Yeah, I mean, listen, I know they rebranded as just engine.com, but previously hotel.
I know them pretty well.
They did an amazing job of getting into the linemen and the electricians and folks working on highways and group bookings in that way.
It was a great go-to-market strategy that they had.
We are in the same space, but very, very different, as you can imagine.
In fact, at least one of my angel investors is an investor in both and knows both quite well.
He's like, you guys are both charging along, but quite differently.

But I think where you can leverage a lot from the content I've seen you guys produce on engine.com is about the overall travel management company space and the basic concept of, hey, you maybe don't need to go use CWT or Amex-GBT.
You can be the first person you're coming
And then others in your company can use it as well.

But the whole understanding of what a travel management company does for you, and the fact that we're not trying to necessarily compete in that space, but we're definitely trying to compete for those employees to come use auto instead of going and using those other systems.

But this is where, again, if I was not working with GrowthX, I might actually build a little AI engine that would go look at engine.com and all their content, pull that in, and then refactor some of it, targeting more towards our type of market, emphasizing the executive assistant for every traveler, emphasizing the fact that for our type of a customer and user, they're not staying for 20 days.
They're
You know, they're going for one meeting and that meeting might get canceled at the last minute or they may add another meeting.
You know, think about your sales folks if they're traveling or if you've ever done any of that.
And some other company says, actually, you know, I can't meet with you tonight, but how about tomorrow morning at 9 a.m.?
The answer is always yes, right?
So now you need someone to move that meeting for you.
And so this is where, you know, we're a little bit more designed and the auto is a little bit more designed for that type of traveler where, frankly,  goes wrong a lot.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
That's super spot on because, like, I don't travel a lot, especially with the two-year-old at home.
But, like, I just got invited to this event from a drone with one our investors and it's just like, okay, we've got to book Seattle.
I haven't been there in a while.
And then it's just like there's a thing in the evening and in the morning the next day and I'm trying to get in and out as quickly as possible, but I also not risk a flight getting canceled.
So we'll build enough buffer on both ends just in case something happens.
And it's like all those things, you know, like.
And Marcel, I'll see you there in three weeks.
Yeah, yeah.
Except I won't be traveling because we're in Seattle, so I'm heading to All right, perfect.
Okay, that'll be fun.
That'll be actually a lot of fun.
Yeah, that's perfect.
So maybe we need to get on the wait list for Otto.
Oh, we can't.
We'll definitely let you guys in right away.
Just send me all the email.
That would be actually awesome because my EA was going to both travel, like, literally today and tomorrow.
Yes, I will send you guys all some invite codes and some links.
Okay, sweet.
Yeah, we're going to do a ton of travel.
You know, this would be a good way for us to learn about the experience so that we have a more and more content strategy for you.
Cool, thank you for that.
This is maybe just a good time to call out, too, like, who our audience is not, right?
I'm kind of stating obvious.
to is...
But to do
Like, when leisure travelers get mixed into the audience and they come into Otto and try to plan a trip to Disney for the family, Otto doesn't do that.
So we just need to be really careful about, you know, getting the right people on the site.
Yeah, and, like, one of the things we do is we create these artifacts is clearly define things like that, the positive but also the negative meaning, like, what we're not, you know.

And so, like, as you nail down any way to talk about your audience, so for instance, like, business travel or working professionals or however you want to name your audience, you know, I think the more we can be consistent with that would be great because then, like, it's super clear everywhere.
The more industry term and certainly the target audience would be the road warrior and different companies define a slightly different, you know, focus wire and...
And SCIFT, which are kind of two research organizations in the travel industry.
And GBTA, the Global Business Travel Association, defined them all slightly differently.
But generally, it's anybody who takes six or more business trips a year.
Which, when I was traveling twice a week, seems like quaint.
But apparently, it's six plus trips per year is a road warrior.
And they're the ones who then tend to start being fairly loyal to an airline.
Because, you know, six trips will generally get you into that first tier.
And you can start getting some benefits from your airline.
Same thing with hotels.
If those six trips are each three or four nights in a hotel, now you're in the 15 plus hotel stays every year.
Which means now you're on Bonvoy's first level and every hotel chain's first level.
You get your free water and you feel special when you check in.
Yeah.
So, but that's kind of the target audience is this road warrior type concept.
Perfect.
Great context.
All right.

So going into, you talked a little bit about it kind of already with some of your customers, you know, just not being happy with the current tools they're using, but are there other kind of trigger events that would lead them to kind of come out and start to search for solutions, products like auto?
I don't know.
And that's, that's part of the kind of marketing issue right now is that this is a, this is a problem.
I don't know that people know there is a solution for the typical solution for somebody who is looking for this.
And, you know, my nephew who is in his mid.

He travels quite a bit for work, and he is also kind of a tech-type guy and went on to Upwork and found an assistant in the Philippines who he kind of gives some tasks to, and he's got that person on kind of a 10-hour-a-week retainer, and he essentially got himself his own executive assistant.
So I talked to him about auto, and he's like, yeah, I'd totally just use that then, right?
Because it's the same thing.

You want to offload all the stuff you don't want to do to someone else, but you're only going to be comfortable with that, and you're only going to do that if you feel that that person understands who you are and understands what you want, right?
So if you hand over your calendar to someone, and they start scheduling you for a 4 to 5 p.m.
every day, and you're like, well, that's...
So when I go to the gym or a 6 to 7 p.m.
meeting every day and you're like, well, that's when I want to be home, then that assistant hasn't gotten to know you and they're not doing their job very well.

And so that's, again, kind of coming back to this whole hyper-personalization, which is it's not personalization of, hey, I know your name, I remember your credit card number, you know, you always like these filters.
But hyper-personalization down to you like staying in hotels that are within a seven-minute walk of where your meeting is going to be.
And it always has to be, you know, X, Y, Z, and your flights never ever take a red eye and ideally sit in, you know, seat 12F when you're flying towards the East Coast.
You know, like you can get that level of personalization and that really becomes that executive assistant concept that really, you know, speaks to what people are looking for.
But it's hard for me to say, is someone going...

To type in and say, I'm looking, I mean, they probably will type in, they're looking for an executive assistant to help them, you know, with their travel or looking for an executive assistant in general.
But I don't think that they'd be looking for, like, they're not going to be typing in travel agent AI, you know, as an idea, right?
The AI is the solution, not the problem.

Yeah, like in the way we think about it is taking this audience first approach, because like by understanding the audience and the nuances of the audience, we can figure out what are all the other surrounding events and triggers and questions and pain points and things where they do need answers, they do need, you know, there are asking questions.
And it might not, like to your point, there's only a very small single digit subset of your audience that's actually searching for AI assistant for doing X, Y, or Z, you know, related to this.
But certainly, I wasn't going to search for, you know, best hotels for blah, blah, blah, blah, Seattle, whatever, right?
Like,
Like, hey, how long should I, does it take to go from the airport and how much should I plan for from Seattle, blah, blah, blah.
Like, again, I'm not horrible.
Like, these are not great examples, but like, those are things that as a user I would do.
And so we're trying to map that a little bit more, you know, that way it might not only get the bullseye because we don't know how to search.
So I can imagine as well, people would type in and say, especially folks who, you know, maybe in their first two years of doing business travel.
They type things in, like, my flight is delayed, I'm going to miss my meeting, what do I do?
You know, or, you know, I don't know, that type of thing where people really are asking.
I think part, really asking, you know, when things go wrong, I think a lot of people just turn to Google because they're on hold with the airline and they're waiting for 45 minutes anyway.
Yeah.
And I think that's, that's a great place to start.

Hey, by the way, if you had booked with Otto, Otto would have seen that your flight is delayed, would have come up with two other options for you, and practically reached out to say, hey, you're not going to make it home tonight.
With Alaska Airlines, I can book you on Delta and you'll end up with a credit on Alaska, and Otto would do that for you.
So I think a lot of times when things go wrong is when people turn to search engines to start asking things.
I think people also who are this road warrior category, they're really into their points, and the status levels, and things like that.
And so Flyertalk, F-L-Y-E-R-T-A-L-K, flyertalk.com, is one of these Reddit-style, or at least message boards, for them,
Autofrequent flyers go to and they'll find out about what promotions are going on.
How do I get extra points?
If I'm gold, can I expect to get an upgrade?
So they're talking about the benefits of business travel without necessarily talking about business travel.
And so, you know, as a small little side thing, pretty early on, we, I'd say we taught Otto how to identify upgradable fares.
Doesn't mean you can go book it with a mileage plan, but if you're, you know, a certain level with United Airlines, they'll give you these, you know, regional upgrade certificates.
And so if you tell Otto, hey, I've got a regional upgrade certificate on RUC, you know, find me a coach fare that I can upgrade on my next flight to New York, Otto will go find that for you.
And so it's those types of things where that's the intersection with.

With this road warrior crowd who is obsessed with flying in the front of the cabin, even though they can only, they're only allowed to fly coached and, you know, they, they want to feel special at the hotel and, and they just want things to work.
Stop describing me, sir.
The thing is, I, I, I lived this life myself for about 10 years doing consulting type work.
And, um, I like, my wife and I travel and I'm doing, going for a mortgage, she's like, why don't we have the status?
I'm like, cause I think we never travel.
It's like, I know, but, but I feel like we should be getting upgraded.
I know.
I'm like, yeah, or we should just pay 10x and buy the more expensive version of everything.
That's right.
Um, any early feedback on kind of.
Fe.
Features that are holding customers back from continuing to try auto or things that are on their future wish list.
So if we think of it as two different categories of customers, there's the customer who's, again, working for this smaller company that does not yet have a travel management company, a TMC.
And therefore, they haven't been told by their CEO or whoever, you have to book on CWT or you have to go book on that, right?
That type of traveler who does not yet have a TMC, who just goes and books on Delta, they go book on Expedia, they go book on Booking.com.
They'll go book directly on Marriott's website.
That type of traveler is in many ways our sweet spot.
But in that case, what we're really replacing is a more directed and a better business travel solution, replacing a leisure website that's been designed for leisure travel, right?
And so a good example of that is when you're traveling for work, you should never, ever buy basic economy seats.
If you accidentally do that, you quickly find out through business travel that you can't pick a seat.
If anything goes wrong, you're not going to be able to change that flight at all.
If you cancel, you won't even get credits for it.
But if you go look on Expedia, that's the price they show you, right?
Because every leisure traveler just wants to see the cheapest.
So in many ways, we're replacing those leisure sites for this type of traveler.
But what we're really doing is giving them the actual flights.
It's the actual hotels that fit what a business traveler would need, pairing that with that hyper-personalization that auto gives every user.

The other type of customer is the one who is working for a company, and they could be small, they could be massive, this could be Google spending close to a billion dollars in travel for their employees a year.
However, if those employees are pretty dissatisfied with the experience that they've got, they'll go act like that other type of a traveler, and they'll go book on their own.

So what usually happens is they have to submit an expense report, and if their manager says, hey, why did you not book on our TMC, our travel management company website, and they give some reason, the only real factor that matters there is, does the manager approve your expense form or not?
At the end, end.
If the manager approves your expense form, you're going to go back to what you prefer.

And so those are the types of customers that I'd like to siphon off as well, are the ones who are dissatisfied with the antiquated design of Concur that was built in 2006, last time it was redesigned type of thing.
You know, or using TripActions, which was the cool company on the block doing travel management, but now also feels pretty antiquated.
And TripActions is now called Navan, N-A-V-A-N.
And so, you know, for that first type of customer and your bullet point of you work for a smaller company, no TMC, I'm not sure that we're missing anything at this point.
For the customers currently working with the TMC, I think what we're missing is expense management.
Integration is a workflow if a company needs that for routing approvals.
But then again, if that employee is able to go on Delta and just book a flight, then the whole approval routing doesn't really matter anyway.
Like I said, it all comes down to, is the manager going to approve the expense report or not?
But I think that's kind of what is missing right now.
The other thing that's holding customers back is a trust in this type of new interaction.

You're effectively, in the customer's mindset, it's the same as them talking to Claude or Perplexity or ChatGPT with the nuance that it's called auto and they can say, hey, I need a hotel and then auto offers to book it.
For them, which is kind of a brand new, unnerving experience for people who've never done that before.
I think the tolerance for errors and inaccuracy is very low for this, too, compared to, like, if I ask ChetJPT something and it gives me a 90% answer, I need, like, perfection with booking a trip.

Yeah, because even in the current, you know, agentic web that OpenAI and Cloud offer, they're still showing you the screen at every decision point that's being made and asking you to confirm, you know, is this the right one?
So the agentic flow with almost no user input is, I'd say, a new interaction type that most people haven't experienced yet.
Yeah.

I think when a lot of people think, and this is even within the technology press and within folks who are very comfortable with AI, including yourselves, when people think of an AI that's going to help you book your travel, they immediately think about more the operator concept is that the AI is going to go browse Expedia for you, right?
But this is not it.
It's literally just going to go hit APIs, pull back your flights, pull back your hotels, just like Expedia would.
But it's not stepping through another company's website.
It's actually doing the transaction for you.
Do you have examples of sales calls or just a little more context and documents of how you do those objectives?
No, because we're not selling to a company.
Right, so there's no sales call per se.
You know, you can think of this as a sales.

Call, you guys are now interested in using it as individuals, but that's kind of the equivalent of a sales call we've had is that, you know, I'm not, you know, there's no way we can scale if we want 10,000 customers.
Places where you aggregate user feedback or they can put in requests and features, things like that would also be helpful.
The more we can ingest, the better.
Okay, we'll see what we can find.
Amazing.
Thank you.
All right.
So I you've filled in a couple of the common objections or concerns, mainly around accuracy.
Can you talk to me a little bit more?
I guess it's mainly user feedback, but it's hard to get user feedback if people are hesitant to use the product.
Yeah, I think what I added there was just kind of the bullet point.
We just talked through, like, hi.
For accuracy and relevance, people are going to bolt if it doesn't make sense, which is what Ava comes back with.
Is there any feedback on security, privacy, especially if they're connecting work emails?

We have not had that as an issue, but I wonder if that's a self-fulfilling thing anyway, because we're asking people to sign up for the beta, and when they sign up with an email address, they're picking whatever email address they want.
We're not requiring a work email address at this point.
They can definitely do that if they can use that if they want.
We have not had anyone who was invited to the beta who sent us any feedback saying, I don't want to sign up because it's too risky or anything like that.
But in the beginning, we...
We...
We're only available with a Gmail login, or it's not Gmail, but a Google authentication.
And then we've now opened it up to, excuse me, opened up to any email address.
And we've got, excuse me, croak my throat.
We've got Apple, Google, and Outlook Exchange or Microsoft login, as well as just regular email.
From a credit card security standpoint, no.
And I can say that with pretty good confidence, we review all of the conversations that are happening at this point, because there's enough that we can actually review them.
And we have AI helping review the conversations as well.
And we're not seeing anything when Otto asks for the credit card details and pops a little modal.
No one is indicating, hey, you know, is this secure?
Why, you know, what, what?
Well, We're
Where do you put my credit card?
And those would be natural things to ask the AI.
But we don't see people asking out of that.
Good signal.
Do you guys use Stripe or something that they know and trust?
No, and that's mainly because of how the travel industry works.
When you transact with any travel agency, they're essentially an agency model in the banking world.
In the banking world, there's agency and there's merchant.
As the agency model, we collect the card information.
We pass it on without giving you the full kind of how travel works.
Think of it as going through three or four different hops and eventually getting to the airline.
The process is your credit card number, all in milliseconds.
But your credit card statement will say United Airlines or Delta.
For hotels, long
A the hotels are pay at hotel, and so you're really just making a reservation with a credit card to hold it, but nobody's processing that.

We are looking to build out content, and so this is something we can send to you guys, but essentially how we secure all this information is, you know, auto the AI and auto the company me and all of our engineers don't have access to nor ever see the credit card information.
We use a company called VGS, very good security.
I don't actually know if we're allowed to use VGS as a name, but you can think of it as a payment vault.

And actually also, you know, all of your personal details, your first and last name, your date of birth, your TSA number, you all those things that you give in order to book a ticket or reserve a hotel room.
It's all...
All stored down, and all we have access to is a token, which represents that private and personalized information.
And so from that standpoint, we don't have to be PCI compliant, or the payment card industry compliant.
We don't have to do any of that because we never actually see or use any of it.
That's the net of it.
Gotcha.
All right.
So where have you guys seen, I guess, the most success from leads or users coming in today out of that?
It's hard to say most success because we kind of are more of a one-trick pony at this point.
So one of the things, you know, in order to a little bit educate users and also to run some ads and try to get more people to come sign up for the beta invite list, the you much.
sort an interesting again.
I think could
Engaged with a company that helped put together some 30-second Instagram videos, and it's kind of the day in the life of a consultant, and if only I'd used Otto, and kind of those types of things.
If you go to ottotheagent as our Instagram handle, you can look at the 15 to 20.
Yeah, that was all right, ottotheagent, there you go.
Just making sure I didn't accidentally text somebody.
If you go there, then you'll see the, I think we're up to 16 or 20 different ads.
Those all just kind of were posted with collaborators, started getting a lot of clicks.
That generated a ton of clicks in to find out more about Otto and people signing up.
And then we switched from, instead of just creating more of these little videos, which at the time were not ads, we've turned those videos now into ads.
there's Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I Yeah.
And our cost per lead of people actually signing or entering their email address, I'd say probably is averaging around $15, $16 per lead.
But we've had weeks, and actually if you look at trailing three weeks, it's closer to $10, $11.
And we don't have enough transactions or traffic yet to understand any statistical significance, how the cost per lead turns into a cost per transaction.
Gotcha.
That makes sense.
So these are pretty much the best piece of content that would be on Instagram so far.
Yep.
Yep.
So primarily Instagram.
Instagram ads, all right.
Let's go back up, see if there's any big ones we skipped over here.
Apologies, I got a drop a little bit earlier, but you guys are in good hands.
All right, thank you.
I'll see you in a few weeks.
I'm excited for that.
All right, so we're getting to the last couple here.
Growth constraints, so any bottlenecks outside of what you listed here, just kind of limited customer usage?
No, I mean, it's artificial on purpose.
It's artificially restrained at this point on purpose.
We need to answer that pretty quick.
We need some fraud validation.
As soon as we got that in, then I can open it up a little bit more.
So, you know, because obviously once you open it up to any good or bad actors on the Internet, then you become a target.
So.
Totally makes sense.
What would or will the process of like content review on the content review side?
Obviously, we communicate a lot on Slack and I'm assuming just send it in that group and both of you take a look at it.
I know a lot of teams have others behind the scenes that will go to their level review.
So just carry on just what that's going look like.
It's going to be the two of us.
I will probably be reviewing more for travel industry accuracy and things like that.
And Jason will be primary on just overall review of any content.
So at the end of the day, if you guys get signed off from Jason, you're good.
If he has any questions or if I poke my head in, I'll probably be looking at the, just the traveling history accuracy type stuff.
Fantastic.
Yeah, and to help with the general accuracy as well, we'll get a deep dive schedule just to look through the product and we'll play around ourselves.
would love either a loom or recording or we can do a live one call as well.
We are, we are working right now and hopefully we'll have something in the next 10 to 14 days on a product video or a couple of product videos.
Love that.
I love that.
Perfect.
It's meant to be.
So just a couple of questions from our side as well, if we are moving to that.
So what is the preferred, I guess, output and, and, and so how do we, how do we actually turn this into an operation?
What I mean by that is.

It's like, do you guys get access to, I remember reading and it's like, you know, treat you like employees of the company, get you access into Slack, get you access into Webflow, et cetera, et cetera.
But, you know, very specifically, are you going to send us a list of things you need?
You know, we're using Webflow today.
If you go look at our public website, it's maybe four pages and has almost nothing on it.
It basically said we got funding and by the way, sign up for the beta here.
Sure, that's about the extent of it.
But to actually activate all of this once we get through certain steps, what should we be thinking about now?
And is there anything else other than feeding you info at this point we should be doing?
Yeah, I think we try to make it as easy as possible when it looks different for each client.
But the baseline is, yes, we access the Google Search Console, Google Analytics, and then the CMS, however you're publishing.
And then what we do is...

Just anything you can throw over the fence, whether that's products, marketing guidelines, brand guidelines, voice style, tone, all of that, we take and ingest and will create artifacts to that that will show you and calibrate on with you.

So the more, the better we've done everything from, for both, you know, we ingested their prompt sessions to better understand how their users were actually using the tool, what they were trying to get done, when they were leaving that data to better inform the templates and the prompts and the content that we created for them.
So on the product and user side, we're happy to ingest any null info or take a look at analyses that you've already done yourself as well.
Okay.
And you guys limit to creating content that then goes onto our pages or do you help structure?
Where the content should live and the hierarchy and what the page should look like.
Do we define the page and page design and then you insert content into it?
And I'm not expecting an answer to all these things, but those are the types of things I'm also curious about.
Yeah, those are great questions.
I love the way you're thinking.
It also varies, but we're happy to provide information for both and for all.
We come with opinions on what we think could work best based on our experience.
We also welcome, you know, it's your baby and your idea and your business.
So we incorporate that as well.
For Obman Code, they kind of gave us full control on this hidden page on their website, the slash guide, to give us full reign on content so we could hit a high velocity of 30 articles per week.
And then the goal there was just let's react based on the early signals and double down on what's working.
You could only do that with velocity and then kind of us having to control over that strategy.
And so I had really, really good results.
results with a month and a half with augmented code because of that.
we've done everyone in between from page structure to slower approach with content and number of articles per week, interactive programmatic plays.

So we've done a lot for clients and we kind of take these first two weeks and then the rest of the sprint, obviously, to better understand what we think will work best, what's the most aligned and what we want to attack first.
Great.
And you guys stick to written content, right?
So you guys aren't, you know, getting into producing product videos and things like that at this time.
So, no, the bread and butter is the written content.

We do, I'll say more to come, more to come and send any cool ideas we have over because we're always also an arm of this company's experimenting with everything that's new and what could, become a more structured, like programmatic play.
come, come, more to
So we've done video before, and we're constantly experimenting.
So feel free to set up your ideas, and we'll be open about capacity if we're able to do it.

I assume it does include an image for each post, though.

Featured image, yeah.
That's great.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Alrighty, well, we're in your hands.

Please do.
We'll bother you if we need things and don't hear back.
Don't worry about us at all.
We'll take anything, and we'll constantly.
Fantastic.
Eric, George, thank you so much for

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Kick off strategy sprint for Otto's content marketing initiative ahead of GA launch.](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Otto is preparing for GA launch in mid-November, transitioning from limited beta to broader availability](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Target audience: frequent business travelers (6+ trips/year) managing own travel, especially at SMBs without corporate travel management](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=431.0)
  - [Content strategy aims to build authority in search/AI, educate on Otto's unique AI assistant approach](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=450.0)
  - [Current lead generation via Instagram video ads (\~$10-15 cost per lead)](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=805.0)

## Topics

### Otto Product Overview

  - [AI-powered executive assistant for booking/managing travel](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=573.0)
  - [Learns user preferences through calendar integration, past bookings](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=746.0)
  - [Focuses on business travelers, not leisure](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=690.0)
  - [Handles common pain points (flight changes, upgrades, personalized recommendations)](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)

### Target Audience & Positioning

  - ["Road warriors": 6+ business trips per year](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1450.0)
  - [Primary segments:](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1549.0)
    1.  SMB employees without corporate travel management
    2.  Dissatisfied users of existing corporate travel tools
  - [Positioning: "Executive assistant for every traveler"](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1134.0)
  - [Not targeting: Leisure travelers, companies with strict travel policies](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1375.0)

### Current Marketing & Growth

  - [Limited beta with hundreds of users](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=396.0)
  - [Main acquisition: Instagram video ads (\~$10-15 cost per lead)](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=805.0)
  - [Artificially constrained growth, pending fraud validation](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2927.0)
  - [Basic website with beta signup, expanding content strategy](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1086.0)

### Content Strategy Goals

  - [Build search/AI authority on business travel topics](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=450.0)
  - [Educate on Otto's unique AI assistant approach](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=450.0)
  - [Address common travel pain points, questions](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1785.0)
  - [Highlight benefits vs. leisure travel sites, outdated corporate tools](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=1042.0)

### Technical & Security Considerations

  - [Uses VGS for secure payment/personal data storage](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2672.0)
  - [Multiple authentication options (Google, Apple, Microsoft, email)](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2521.0)
  - [No reported user concerns about security/privacy](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2548.0)

### GrowthX Engagement

  - [8-week strategy sprint to accelerate content development](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2315.0)
  - [Will provide access to analytics, CMS, brand guidelines](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2104.0)
  - [Flexible on content structure, page hierarchy based on Otto's needs](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2181.0)
  - [Primary focus on written content, open to exploring other formats](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=3281.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Otto to provide access to analytics, CMS, brand guidelines](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2104.0)
  - [GrowthX to ingest product info, user data, create strategy artifacts](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2104.0)
  - [Schedule deep-dive product demo (Otto creating product videos in 2 weeks)](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2976.0)
  - [Otto to send GrowthX team beta access for hands-on experience](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2104.0)
  - [Ongoing communication via Slack for content reviews, strategy alignment](https://fathom.video/share/iPVsU_yRUVkt6NdKPKyG4KXxW5sTcn_5?tab=summary&timestamp=2104.0)

action_items.description: Send Otto invite codes to GrowthX team (Erik, George, Marcel)
